meta.title: My Hero - Grohl Rhythm Engine
meta.subtitle: Add palm muting and dynamics to make your riff breathe.

objectives: Use palm muting to create tight verse-style parts
Open up your strumming for big chorus moments
Control dynamics to tell a "quiet-loud" story

steps.title: What makes Dave Grohl sound huge?
steps.body: A lot of the magic in My Hero is not complicated notes - it's the way the rhythm guitar moves between tight, controlled verses and exploding choruses. In this lesson you'll use palm muting and dynamics to turn your power-chord loop into something that feels much more like the real song.

steps.title: Quick palm-muting review
steps.body: Rest the side of your picking hand lightly on the strings near the bridge, right where the strings meet the metal. Strum your low-string power chord. You should hear a chunky, "chug-chug" sound instead of a full ring. If it's too dead, move your hand a little closer to the neck. If it's too bright, move slightly toward the bridge.

steps.title: Verse: tight and muted
steps.body: Take the power-chord My Hero loop from the previous lesson and play it with palm muting the entire time. Use steady 8th-note downstrokes but keep the sound controlled and low to the strings. Imagine you're playing along with a drummer on the kick and snare. This is your verse feel.

steps.title: Chorus: open and huge
steps.body: Now play the same loop again but lift your palm completely off the strings. Let the chords ring out fully and strum a little harder. Keep the rhythm identical - same tempo, same 8th-note grid - but let the sound get much bigger. This contrast between muted and open is what makes the chorus explode.

steps.title: Add backbeat accents
steps.body: While you're playing your 8th-note groove, try hitting beats 2 and 4 a little harder than the others. Count "1 2 3 4" and make 2 and 4 pop out. This locks you in with the snare drum and gives the riff a stronger, more professional feel.

steps.title: Smooth verse-to-chorus changes
steps.body: Practice playing 2 bars of muted "verse" riff followed by 2 bars of open "chorus" riff. The only thing that changes is your right-hand touch. Your timing and strumming motion stay exactly the same. No pauses, no tempo jumps - just a smooth lift from quiet to loud.

steps.title: Your own quiet-loud engine
steps.body: Can you loop 2 bars muted and 2 bars open for at least 4 cycles in a row without losing the beat? Listen for a clear difference between the "verse" sound (tight, controlled) and "chorus" sound (big, ringing). If you can feel that contrast, you're starting to think like a rock rhythm guitarist.

steps.title: 🎸 You control the energy!
steps.body: Great job! You just learned how to shape a riff using palm muting, open strums, and accents - the same tools players like Dave Grohl rely on. You're not just playing the right chords anymore - you're controlling the energy of the song.
